Omics_layers: Transcriptomics; Epigenomics; Proteomics; Metabolomics; Lipidomics; Genomics
Technologies: RNA-seq,  microarray; DNA methylation, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq; LC-MS proteomics, Olink, CyTOF; Metabolomics; Lipidomics; SNPs, CNVs
Strengths: Handles missing data; interpretable latent factors
Limitations: Linear relationship assumption; sensitive to pre-processing and normalization
Output_formats: Latent factors, feature loadings, variance explained, factor–covariate associations

Use_case: Unsupervised temporal integration, latent factor discovery
Technologies: RNA-seq, microarray; DNA methylation, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq; LC-MS proteomics, Olink; Metabolomics; Lipidomics
Strengths: Models continuous covariates (age, dose, time, space); captures smooth temporal trajectories; interpretable latent factors
Limitations: Requires continuous covariates; assumes smooth variation
Best_for: Time course experiments, developmetal and ageing trajectories
Input_formats: Multi-view sample × feature matrices + continuous covariates
Input_details: Paired samples across omics layers; temporal, age or dosage values
Output_formats: Latent factors, feature loadings, variance explained, temporal trajectories, uncertainty estimates
Underlying_method: Bayesian group factor analysis + Gaussian processes

Strengths: Integrates datasets with an outcome variable

Omics_layers: Transcriptomics; proteomics; metabolomics; lipidomics; microbiome; metagenomics
Technologies: RNA-seq; 16S; shotgun metagenomics; microarray; mass spectrometry-based proteomics; metabolomic
Strengths: Performs multivariate integration and discriminant analysis
Limitations: Linear relationship between different sets of data
Best_for: Identify biomarkers across independent omics studies
Preprocessing_steps: Normalization; recommended pre-filtering the data to less than 10K predictors per data set

Technologies: Array comparative genomic hybridization; gene expression microarray; RNA-Seq; DNA-Seq; WES; WGS
Strengths: Integrates discrete and continuous variables; allows selection of reliable and unreliable features
Limitations: Assumption of normality distribution for continuous variables; complex choiche of best model through penalized linear regression; no handling of missing values; computationally expensive for large datasets due to  hyperparameter tuning
Best_for: Integrative clustering of multiple genomic data types; Studies that incorportate both CNV analysis and gene expression data
Preprocessing_steps: Heavy preprocessing: binarization of somatic mutations, gene selection for expression data, DNAcopy package to analyze CNVs
Input_details: Samples x genes matrices and hyperparameter setting
Underlying_method: Penalized linear latent variable model / Bayesian latent variable model

Strengths: Couples multiple set of features measured on the same set of samples. Since it does not rely on feature annotation, it is not limited by the immaturity of annotations. There is no prerequisite to filter or map features (genes) to a common set thereby considerably increasing genome coverage.
Limitations: Selects more features; interpretation would have to be coupled with other methods, such as enrichment analysis,
Best_for:
Applied when several set of variables (genes, transcripts,
proteins) are measured on the same set of individuals
Preprocessing_steps: microarray (normalisation, log transformation); proteomics (log transformed); RNA-seq (RPKM, RSEM, normalisation, log transformation)
Input_details: Oaired samples accross different omics matrices

Technologies: RNA-seq, Olink, Mass Spectrometry Proteomics; Mass Spectrometry Phosphoproteomics, Lipidomics
Strengths: Can operate with missing data across omics layers; Enables differential network analysis for single or multiomic data; easy to visualise cross-omics patterns; when used for feature selection leads to interpretable ML models
Limitations: Linear relationship assumption; requires preprocessing (normalization, batch effect correction) prior to analysis; no imputation or sample exclusion performed internally; lower edge stability for omics layers with limited samples
Best_for: Identifying differential interactions across omics layers for biomarker discovery; feature engineering in machine learning pipelines for predictive modeling; interactive exploration of multiomic networks.
Preprocessing_steps: Normalization, Batch effect correction and quality checks must be applied prior to analysis. No internal preprocessing or imputation is performed.
Input_details: List of preprocessed omics matrices with appropriate normalization and quality checks
Output_formats: Differential networks (visualized interactively); S3 model objects containing selected features for machine learning; Statistical significance metrics (P values, adjusted P values); Differential regression plots
Underlying_method: Linear regression with interaction terms (ordinary least-squares or robust linear regression) to assess differential connectivity; percolation analysis for adaptive thresholding; ANCOVA for multigroup comparisons.

Use_case: Feature selection & interpretation; feature importance, biomarker discovery, model interpretation in supervised learning
Integration_type: XGBoost is a supervised machine learning algorithm; SHAP is a model explanation technique; Supervised (XGBoost model) + post-hoc explainability
Omics_layers: Genomics, Proteomics, Transcriptomcis, Metabolomics + clinical data
Technologies: RNA-seq; microarray; SNP arrays; LC-MS; clinical tabular data
Strengths: Model-agnostic (works with many ML models); Provides local + global feature importance; Highly interpretable (Shapley values grounded in game theory); Suitable for biomarker discovery
Limitations: Computationally expensive for large datasets; Assumes feature independence (can affect interpretation); Not an integration method itself (relies on trained model)
Best_for: Explaining predictions from XGBoost models; Identifying important clinical/omics features driving outcomes
Study_examples: Applications in aging, cancer, and immune response studies (no single canonical inflammaging paper, but widely used for biomarker interpretation)
Preprocessing_steps: Normalization for omics data; Feature selection (optional for high dimensional data); Encoding categorical variables
Input_details: Numerical features required; Works with combined multi-omics + clinical feature matrices
Output_formats: SHAP values (per feature per sample); Summary plots, dependence plots, force plots
Underlying_method: Shapley values from cooperative game theory applied to ML predictions

Use_case: Bulk multi-omics clustering; cancer subtyping; handles partial datasets (patients with missing omics layers) without imputation
Omics_layers: Gene expression (RNA-seq) + DNA methylation + miRNA expression; any bulk omics combination
Strengths: Handles partial multi-omic data without imputation; no iterative optimization (fast, ~10s runtime); simple algorithm; competitive with state-of-the-art on full data; outperforms alternatives on partial data
Limitations: No downstream tasks beyond cluster assignment; spectral clustering bottleneck O(n³) for large cohorts; not actively maintained (last update 2018)
Best_for: Cancer subtyping from bulk multi-omics cohorts; studies with incomplete omic coverage across patients (partial data)
Study_examples: 10 TCGA cancer datasets (AML, BIC, COAD, GBM, KIRC, LIHC, LUSC, SKCM, OV, SARC; n=3168 patients); AML partial multi-omic dataset
Preprocessing_steps: Normalized feature-by-sample matrices per omic; same feature set per omic across all samples; partial data allowed
Input_formats: R data frames / matrices (one per omic)
Input_details: Feature-by-sample matrices (e.g. genes × patients), one per omic, stored as a list; partial data supported (not all patients need all omics)
Output_formats: Cluster assignment vector (one label per patient); integrated affinity graph (n×n matrix)
Underlying_method: k-nearest neighbour similarity per omic → relative similarity matrices → average relative similarity → spectral clustering (eigengap-based k selection)
